SOX (Sarbanes-Oxley Act) refers to a United States federal law that was enacted in 2002 to improve corporate governance, financial reporting, and auditing of publicly traded companies. It is named after its sponsors, Senator Paul Sarbanes and Representative Michael Oxley. 

The Sarbanes-Oxley Act was introduced in response to major accounting scandals, such as Enron and WorldCom, in order to protect investors and restore public trust in the financial markets. It sets guidelines and requirements for corporate financial disclosures, internal controls, and the independence of auditors.

In summary, SOX aims to promote transparency, accuracy, and accountability in financial reporting by establishing standards and regulations for publicly traded companies in the United States.
